items.question: How does a partial derivative ∂f/∂x differ from an ordinary derivative df/dx for f(x, y)?
items.answer: The partial derivative holds y (and all other independent variables) fixed and measures sensitivity only along the x-axis slice. The ordinary derivative df/dx applies when x is the sole independent variable or when you account for how all variables change with x via the total derivative and chain rule.

items.question: How do partial derivatives relate to the gradient of f(x, y)?
items.answer: The gradient ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y) collects all first-order partials into one vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent. Individual partials are the components; the gradient adds direction and magnitude in the full input space.

items.question: What if you set ∂f/∂x = 0 and ∂f/∂y = 0 at a point (a, b)?
items.answer: The point is a critical point (stationary in all coordinate directions): the tangent plane is horizontal. It may be a local minimum, maximum or saddle point; second partials or the Hessian determine which.

items.question: What if you increase only weight wᵢ by a small ε while fixing all other parameters in a loss L(θ)?
items.answer: The loss changes by approximately ε·(∂L/∂wᵢ) to first order. Gradient descent uses this sensitivity, updating wᵢ ← wᵢ − η·(∂L/∂wᵢ) to decrease L locally.
